title: CKA, SVCCA, and representation similarity
learningObjectives: Explain cka, svcca, and representation similarity in plain language
Write down the objective and identify its assumptions
Design one test that could falsify an over-strong interpretation

whyThisLesson.text: Similarity metrics compare geometry or subspaces across models and runs. They do not establish one-to-one correspondence between features. This lesson gives you a concrete test for that claim before the course moves to the next abstraction.

intuition.analogy: Evaluation is a medical panel, not a single thermometer. One number can be reassuring while another reveals the problem. Similarity metrics compare geometry or subspaces across models and runs. They do not establish one-to-one correspondence between features.
intuition.analogyLimit: No single metric measures interpretability. Every metric needs an explicit non-claim beside it.
intuition.text: Similarity metrics compare geometry or subspaces across models and runs. They do not establish one-to-one correspondence between features.

toyExample.description: Run two methods across several seeds and plot fidelity, sparsity, similarity, and intervention effects separately instead of collapsing them into one score.

steps.result: Specify exactly what cka, svcca, and representation similarity should recover, preserve, or ignore.
steps.insight: The intended factor must be known before a toy result can be judged.

steps.result: Use the same data, capacity, training budget, and evaluation examples for both methods.
steps.insight: Otherwise a visible gain may be a resource difference in disguise.

steps.result: Vary the source correlation, noise, view quality, or sparsity and record the first failure.
steps.insight: The boundary of success is usually more useful than the headline score.

visual.question: Which assumption behind cka, svcca, and representation similarity changes the result most?
visual.description: Move one control at a time. Treat the display as a toy model, then write down the real experiment it suggests.

technical.text: Similarity metrics compare geometry or subspaces across models and runs. They do not establish one-to-one correspondence between features. In practice, write down the input unit, the operation applied to it, and the exact statistic used to score the result. Those are different things. A learned representation can be useful even when the interpretation attached to it turns out to be wrong.

mathematics.explanation: For this lesson, the expression makes the central trade-off explicit. Similarity metrics compare geometry or subspaces across models and runs. They do not establish one-to-one correspondence between features.
mathematics.whyNeeded: It says precisely what the method is being rewarded for.
mathematics.ifRemoved: You could not tell whether two implementations were solving the same problem.
mathematics.encourages: A testable prediction about the chosen objective.
mathematics.doesNotGuarantee: No single metric measures interpretability. Every metric needs an explicit non-claim beside it.

methodComparison.question: What does the metric measure?
methodComparison.practicalImplication: Report the metric's non-claim beside its result.

evidence.claim: Similarity metrics compare geometry or subspaces across models and runs. They do not establish one-to-one correspondence between features.
evidence.note: Course explanation. Use the research library's primary sources for paper-specific empirical claims.

failureModes.name: A score that answers the wrong question
failureModes.mechanism: The objective rewards a proxy while the paper describes a stronger scientific property.
failureModes.symptom: The headline metric rises, but seed stability, controls, or interventions disagree.
failureModes.diagnosticTest: Add a matched negative control and one counterfactual intervention.
failureModes.mitigation: Either narrow the claim or add the missing measurement.
failureModes.setconcaRelevance: Report both the intended benefit and the most plausible shortcut for every SetConCA loss term.

setconcaConnection.relevance: CKA, SVCCA, and representation similarity determines how SetConCA should represent, group, or evaluate activation views.
setconcaConnection.whichPart: The choice of sparse code, view set, coordination loss, or evaluation.
setconcaConnection.whichDesignChoice: Match the pointwise SAE and SetConCA on model, activation site, width, sparsity budget, and compute.
setconcaConnection.whichFailure: Mistaking stronger agreement for recovery of the intended concept.
setconcaConnection.whichExperiment: Compare fidelity, feature stability, selectivity, and controlled interventions; report each result separately.
setconcaConnection.alternativeExplanation: An apparent gain may come from extra context, effective capacity, or easier positives rather than set coordination.

exercises.prompt: A paper claims that cka, svcca, and representation similarity improved interpretability. What one result would you ask for before accepting that claim?
exercises.reveal: Ask for the smallest missing control: a matched baseline, several seeds, a negative control, or an intervention that tests the claimed mechanism.

masteryQuestions.prompt: Explain cka, svcca, and representation similarity to a colleague, then name one thing the method cannot establish by itself.

nextLesson.bridge: Next: Stability across seeds and models. The next idea reuses this lesson's representation and makes a new assumption explicit.

fields.label: Falsifiable claim
fields.prompt: Write the smallest claim this lesson supports.

fields.label: Control
fields.prompt: What control would rule out a shortcut?
